title: Functions
description: Functions are the building blocks of every interview solution. Master TypeScript's typed parameters, return types, arrow functions, and generics to write clear, type-safe code under pressure.

sections.title: Typed parameters and return types
sections.explanation: In TypeScript, annotate every parameter and the return type. This catches bugs before you run the code — passing the wrong type to a function is a common interview mistake. The syntax is `function name(param: Type): ReturnType`.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `multiply` that takes two numbers and returns their product. Write a function `repeat` that takes a string and a count, returning the string repeated count times. Print multiply(6, 7) and repeat("ab", 3).
exercise.hint: multiply returns number, repeat returns string. Fill in the return type annotations after the closing parenthesis.

sections.title: Arrow functions with types
sections.explanation: Arrow functions are concise and commonly used as callbacks. In TypeScript, the type annotation syntax is the same. For single-expression bodies, you can omit the braces and return keyword. Interviewers expect you to use arrows fluently in sort comparators, map callbacks, etc.

exercise.prompt: Write an arrow function `double` that takes a number and returns it doubled. Write an arrow function `isPositive` that takes a number and returns a boolean. Print double(21) and isPositive(-5).
exercise.starterCode:
const double = (n: number): ___ => n * 2

const isPositive = (n: number): ___ => n > 0

console.log(double(21))
console.log(isPositive(-5))
exercise.hint: double returns number, isPositive returns boolean. Arrow functions with single expressions don't need braces.

sections.title: Optional and default parameters
sections.explanation: Optional parameters use `?` and default parameters use `= value`. In interviews, these are useful for functions that take optional configuration like a starting index or initial value. Optional parameters must come after required ones.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `pad` that takes a string, a target length, and an optional fill character (default " "). Pad the string on the right to reach target length. Print pad("hi", 5) and pad("hi", 5, ".").

validation.message: Default pads with spaces, override pads with dots
Use a default parameter for the fill character

exercise.hint: Set the default fill to " " (space). The while loop appends fill until result.length reaches len.

sections.title: Generics basics
sections.explanation: Generics let you write functions that work with any type while preserving type safety. The syntax `<T>` introduces a type parameter. This is critical for writing reusable data structure helpers in interviews — a single `swap<T>` works for numbers, strings, or any type.

exercise.prompt: Write a generic function `lastElement<T>` that takes a `T[]` and returns the last element (or undefined if empty). Print the result for [1,2,3] and ["x","y"].
exercise.hint: Access arr[arr.length - 1] to get the last element. The generic <T> makes this work for any element type.
